title: The role of tears in completing the stress response cycle

htmlContent:
What your body is actually doing when you cry

I used to think crying was weakness. A failure of control. Something to hide, apologize for, push down until the pressure passed. Maybe you learned the same thing. Maybe someone told you to stop crying or you'd "really have something to cry about." Maybe you swallowed so many tears that you forgot what it felt like to let them fall.

Here's the thing. Tears are not a sign that something is wrong with you. They are a sign that something is working. Right on schedule.

Stephen Porges, the neuroscientist who developed Polyvagal Theory, spent decades mapping how your nervous system responds to threat and safety. He talks about the social engagement system, the vagus nerve, and how your body is constantly scanning for cues of danger or connection. But what many people miss is this: tears are part of your body's built-in completion mechanism. They are not the problem. They are the solution your system has been waiting for.

Think about it. When an animal escapes a predator, it shakes. Trembles. Spends the energy that was mobilized for fight or flight. Then it goes back to grazing. The stress response cycle completes. Humans, though? We override that. We freeze. We hold our breath. We clench our jaws and tell ourselves to get it together. And the incomplete cycle stays lodged in our tissues, waiting for a chance to finish.

Tears are one of the most direct ways your body completes that cycle. They are not random. They are physiological. They contain stress hormones, literally flushing them from your system. A 1983 study by biochemist William Frey showed that emotional tears carry different chemical compositions than reflex tears from cutting an onion. Emotional tears contain higher levels of protein, manganese, and the stress hormone ACTH. Your body is not just leaking water. It is actively detoxifying from the stress response.

Wild, right? But here's where it gets complicated.

Why most of us learned to stop tears

You were not born ashamed of crying. Babies cry freely. They cry when hungry, tired, scared, overwhelmed. They cry and someone comes. That is the original template: distress, expression, relief, connection. But somewhere along the way, most of us got the message that tears are inconvenient, manipulative, or dangerous. Maybe you were told to "toughen up." Maybe your tears made your parents uncomfortable, so they tried to stop them. Maybe you learned that crying led to punishment, not comfort.

That learning gets encoded in your nervous system. Your body starts to associate tears with shame, rejection, or danger. So when the impulse to cry arises, your system slams on the brakes. You tighten your throat. You shallow-breathe. You dissociate slightly, pulling your awareness away from your body. The tears don't come. The cycle doesn't complete. The stress chemicals stay put.

Gabor Mate, a physician who has written extensively about the connection between stress and illness, talks about this directly. In his book When the Body Says No (paid link), he explores how suppressed emotion and incomplete stress responses contribute to chronic disease. He doesn't blame people for their illnesses. He shows how the body's attempts to protect us can backfire over time. Tears that never fall don't just disappear. They get stored. They get converted into tension patterns, autoimmune flares, migraines, digestive issues. The body keeps the score, as Bessel van der Kolk put it.

I am not saying every illness comes from unprocessed tears. That would be simplistic and cruel. But I am saying that when you chronically suppress the completion of the stress response cycle, you are asking your body to hold something it was never designed to hold. And eventually, something has to give.

Sit with that for a moment. Notice what just happened in your body when you read those words. Did your chest tighten? Did your throat close? Did you feel a sudden urge to look away from the screen? That is your nervous system responding to the possibility of vulnerability. That is the part of you that learned to protect yourself from tears.

How tears actually complete the cycle

Let me walk you through what happens physiologically when you allow yourself to cry. Not the controlled, single-tear-rolling-down-your-cheek kind of crying. The messy, ugly, snotty, heaving kind. The kind you probably avoid at all costs.

First, your breathing changes. You start to take deeper, more irregular breaths. This is your body overriding the shallow, constricted breathing of the freeze response. Your diaphragm moves. Your vagus nerve gets stimulated. Your heart rate variability shifts toward a more flexible, resilient pattern. You are literally re-regulating your nervous system through the act of crying.

Second, your body releases oxytocin and endogenous opioids. These are the same neurochemicals that flood your system during bonding, childbirth, and orgasm. They are pain-relieving, connection-promoting chemicals. They are why you often feel a sense of calm or even exhaustion after a good cry. Your body is rewarding you for completing the cycle.

Third, your tears carry out stress hormones. As I mentioned earlier, emotional tears contain ACTH and other byproducts of the stress response. When you cry, you are literally excreting the chemical residue of your stress. You are not wallowing. You are not being dramatic. You are doing housekeeping.

Fourth, crying often leads to shaking or trembling. This is another completion mechanism. Peter Levine, the creator of Somatic Experiencing, has written extensively about how trembling and crying work together to discharge stored survival energy. In his book Waking the Tiger (paid link), he describes how animals in the wild shake after escaping predators, completing the cycle that humans so often interrupt. Crying and shaking are siblings. They work best together.

So why does it feel so hard to let this happen? Because your nervous system has learned that crying is unsafe. And unlearning that takes time.

Creating conditions for tears to come

You cannot force tears. If you sit down and say "I am going to cry now," your system will probably resist. Tears come when your nervous system feels safe enough to release what it has been holding. The key word is safe. You cannot command your body to complete the stress response cycle. You can only create the conditions for it to happen on its own.

What does that look like practically? First, you need to lower the threat level in your environment. That might mean finding a private space. It might mean wrapping yourself in a blanket, turning down the lights, or putting on music that feels safe. It might mean having a trusted person nearby who will not try to fix you or stop the tears.

Second, you need to tolerate the sensations that come before crying. The heat in your face. The lump in your throat. The tightness in your chest. These are not emergencies. They are signals that your system is preparing to release. Most of us panic at these sensations and try to distract ourselves. We check our phones. We get up and make tea. We change the subject. Stay with me here. If you can stay present with those pre-cry sensations for even thirty seconds, you give your system a chance to move through them.

Third, you might need to support your body's sensory needs. Some people find that noise sensitivity makes it harder to drop into the vulnerable state needed for tears. A discreet option for reducing noise sensitivity is Loop Quiet 2 Ear Plugs (paid link). They lower ambient noise without blocking it completely, which can help your nervous system feel contained enough to let go.

Fourth, you can use movement to prepare the ground. A tool that many find helpful for releasing stored body tension is TriggerPoint GRID Foam Roller (paid link). Rolling out your back, hips, and shoulders before attempting emotional release can loosen the physical armor that keeps tears locked in. The body holds memory in tissue. Sometimes you need to soften the tissue before the tears can flow.

And if the tears still do not come? That is okay. That is information. Your system is telling you that it does not feel safe enough yet. Honor that. Do not pathologize it. Keep building safety. Keep showing up. The tears will come when they are ready.

The uncomfortable truth about crying and recovery

Here is what no one tells you about completing the stress response cycle through tears. It does not feel good in the moment. It feels terrible. It feels like you are falling apart. It feels like the grief, rage, or despair you have been holding will never end. And that is exactly why most people stop before the cycle completes.

Your nervous system will try to pull you out of the experience. It will send thoughts like "this is pointless" or "you are being ridiculous" or "you have cried enough, time to get up and do something productive." Those thoughts are not truth. They are resistance. They are the protective parts of you that learned long ago that tears were dangerous.

Janina Fisher, a trauma therapist who integrates Internal Family Systems and sensorimotor psychotherapy, talks about how parts of us will try to stop emotional release because they believe it will destabilize us. But here is the paradox. Suppressing the cycle is what keeps you stuck. Completing it is what allows you to stabilize at a new level of regulation.

I am not saying you should cry all the time. I am not saying tears are the only way to complete the stress response cycle. Laughter, shaking, yawning, sweating, and deep breathing all play a role. Deb Dana, who translates Polyvagal Theory into clinical practice, emphasizes that we need a range of ventral vagal resources to regulate effectively. But tears are one of the most direct and underutilized tools we have. They are free. They are natural. They are your body's way of saying "I am done holding this."

So I will leave you with this. The next time you feel tears coming, notice what happens in your body. Notice the impulse to stop them. Notice the stories you tell yourself about why you should not cry. And then, if you can, if it is safe enough, let them come. Let them be ugly. Let them be messy. Let them last as long as they need to. Your body knows how to complete the cycle. It has been waiting for permission.
